coredata.dc:title: Rapid statistical discrimination of fluorescence images of multiple foodborne pathogens using multicolor-emissive carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Hang
Yao, Shuo
Wang, Xuechen
Xu, Hui
Zhao, Chao
Li, Juan
Wang, Juan
coredata.dc:description: The rapid discrimination of multiple foodborne pathogens is vital and challengeable in ensuring food safety. Herein, a facile fluorescence method was developed to distinguish different bacteria using multicolor-emissive carbon dots (CDs) based on confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and linear discriminant analysis (LDA). The array employed single CDs as receptors and indicators, two pH solutions and three excitation channels as sensing elements for multicolor imaging and recognition of multiple bacteria. Through one-step staining, the CDs were capable of rapid adsorbing onto all bacteria in 1 minute with varying affinities. The discrimination ability of the array was markedly enhanced by means of the synergistic recognition of bacteria and CDs across diverse pH levels and three excitation channels. By using LDA, six kinds of bacteria could be well discriminated with the classification accuracy of 100%. Meanwhile, the feasibility has been successfully expanded to mixture and food samples discrimination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Foodborne pathogen
Carbon dots
Multicolor fluorescence
CLSM
LDA

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and structural characteristics of CDs
Applicability of the bacterial identification array

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Plan Item
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 82073557 , 82273686 and 82373628 ), Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Plan Item (Grant No. 20230203153SF ), Program of “Medicine + X” Interdisciplinary Innovation Team of Bethune Medical Department (Grant No. 2022JBGS09 ) and Norman Bethune Program of Jilin University ( 2023B37 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the CDs, (b) FTIR spectrum of the CDs, (c) XPS spectra of full scan spectra, (d-f) high-resolution C1s, N1s, O1s spectra of CDs.

(a) FL emission spectra of CDs at different excitation, (b) the FL intensity of CDs at different concentrations with various excitation wavelength, (c) FL decay curves of different concentrations of CDs.

Canonical score plots for optimization of the pH of BR buffer for bacteria staining. (a-e) pH = 7, pH = 3 and 7, pH = 4 and 7, pH = 5 and 7, pH = 6 and 7, (f) Jackknifed classification matrix obtained using LDA for different combinations of pH for the 6 bacteria.

Confocal images of six kinds of bacteria stained with 1.16 mg·mL−1 CDs under different pH (a) pH = 3, (b) pH = 7 with excitations at 405 nm, 488 nm and 561 nm, respectively, (c) the FL fingerprints model of different bacteria based on multicolor-emissive CDs, (d) FL response levels of six sensing elements (pH 3 - B, pH 3 - G, pH 3 - R, pH 7 - B, pH 7 - G, and pH 7 - R) for different bacteria. Canonical score plot for distinguishing between bacteria based on CDs with LDA (e) P7E, (f) P37E.

Confocal images of a mixture of bacteria stained with CDs (insetgreen arrows - L. monocytogenes, red arrows - V. parahemolyticus, gray arrows - S. aureus) with excitations of (a) 405 nm, (b) 488 nm and (c) 561 nm, respectively, (d) canonical score plot for distinguishing bacteria mixture based on CDs with LDA (blue polygonal star represent the chosen bacteria).

Canonical score plot for distinguishing between bacteria in food samples based on CDs with LDA. (a) E. coli O157:H7 in milk sample, (b) E. coli O157:H7 in pork sample, (c) L. monocytogenes in milk sample, (d) L. monocytogenes in pork sample, (e) S. aureus in milk sample, (f) S. aureus in pork sample, (g) S. bogdii in milk sample, (h) S. bogdii in pork sample, (i) S. typhimurium in milk sample (j) S. typhimurium in pork sample, (k) V. parahemolyticus in salted vegetable sample, (l) V. parahemolyticus in clam sample.
Schematic diagram for rapidly statistical discrimination of fluorescence images of multiple foodborne pathogens using multicolor-emissive carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Rapid statistical discrimination of fluorescence images of multiple foodborne pathogens using multicolor-emissive carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Public Health, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid discrimination of multiple foodborne pathogens is vital and challengeable in ensuring food safety. Herein, a facile fluorescence method was developed to distinguish different bacteria using multicolor-emissive carbon dots (CDs) based on confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and linear discriminant analysis (LDA). The array employed single CDs as receptors and indicators, two pH solutions and three excitation channels as sensing elements for multicolor imaging and recognition of multiple bacteria. Through one-step staining, the CDs were capable of rapid adsorbing onto all bacteria in 1 minute with varying affinities. The discrimination ability of the array was markedly enhanced by means of the synergistic recognition of bacteria and CDs across diverse pH levels and three excitation channels. By using LDA, six kinds of bacteria could be well discriminated with the classification accuracy of 100%. Meanwhile, the feasibility has been successfully expanded to mixture and food samples discrimination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne diseases attributed to pathogenic bacteria have emerged as a global public health concern, afflicting more than 600 million people nowadays, constituting serious menace to human well-being and inflicting huge financial losses on countries [1–3]. Due to the complexity of the food matrix, most bacteria rarely exist alone in a single food or food raw material, generally more than one coexist. Multiple bacterial contamination may cause more detrimental health problems in humans and simultaneous detection of multiple bacteria is still a huge challenge and research hotpot [4–7]. Currently, the existing approaches for detecting multiple bacteria, such as bacterial culture and multiplex polymerase chain reaction (mPCR) have proven to be highly efficacious reliable. Nonetheless, culture-based techniques necessitate require various selective medium and lengthy incubation time (over 48 h), mPCR-based methods need the intricate pre-processing steps and probecoding analysis, imposing the drawbacks of being laborious and time-intensive [8,9]. Therefore, it is urgent for the development of an alternative assay for rapidly, simultaneously and accurately identifying and detecting pathogenic bacteria with high throughput.

Recently, array-based nonspecific cross-response strategies have been developed to address the issue, in which several semi-selective recognition elements are simply designed to response multiple analytes with subtle changes [10–12]. The detectable signals are processed by multivariate statistical analysis of machine learning based on pattern recognition, such as linear discriminant analysis (LDA). Resorting to learning the model from a part of data, LDA seeks a set of dimensions that best divides unknown species into known species, and various targets can be effectively identified and classified through generating different regions with clear boundaries. So far, numerous sensor arrays have been developed to detect amino acid, proteins, biothiols and so on, proving array-based method a robust technique for profiling intricate samples with high throughput [13–15]. However, there are still few studies on simultaneous differentiation of multiple bacteria.

Up to now, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant interest as an emerging fluorescent nanomaterial in the detection and imaging of biomolecules. On the one hand, CDs have attributes that make them promising transducing elements, for instance, high quantum yield, exceptional photostability, as well as unique optical response to the local environment (excitation, pH, temperature and solvent medium)[16–19]. What’s more, CDs can be easily synthesized and functionalized in large quantities, renowned for the tunable surface chemistry, making CDs be valued as recognition elements for bacteria. For examples, cis-diol group-containing saccharides on bacterial surface can be covalently identified by boronic acid-functionalized CDs, glycopeptide antibiotics-functionalized CDs exhibit strong binding affinity towards D-Ala-D-Ala dipeptide on bacterial cell walls [20]. Additionally, CDs functionalized with various surface charges and hydrophobic/hydrophilic moieties can also be utilized for bacterial identification through non-covalent interactions (including electrostatic interactions and/or hydrophobic interactions)[21]. Satisfactory discrimination performance has been demonstrated by CDs-equipped sensor arrays in the detection of multiple bacteria in complex samples [22–24]. However, due to the high demand of a variety of receptors and the high precision instrument, the simple construction of sensor arrays and the reduction of detection time are quite challenging.

Thus, in this work, we utilized the multicolor-emissive CDs with excitation and concentration-dependent fluorescence (FL) property for multiple bacteria imaging and identification. The principle relies on utilizing two pH BR solutions and three excitation channels as the array elements and CDs as versatile receptors and signal indicators. The CDs could rapidly bind to all the bacteria (1 minute) and exhibited various affinities to different bacteria due to the differences in physicochemical properties and structure of bacterial surfaces. By exploiting disparities in the emission intensity observed in bacteria stained with CDs in different pH environment at three excitation wavelengths via confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) technique, it became feasible to generate distinctive fingerprint maps for six kinds of bacteria. And the differentiation of bacteria could be achieved through the machine learning method of LDA (Scheme 1 ). Furthermore, the feasibility for recognizing the bacteria in mixtures and complex food samples was also validated.

The CDs was synthesized by hydrothermal process following a previous method [25]. Typically, 0.2 g citric acid, 0.36 g PEI and 15 mL water was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was then subjected to a temperature of 200 ℃ for a duration of 12 h. Subsequently, the product underwent natural cooling followed by centrifugation at 15,000 rpm for 15 min, the supernatant showed yellowish-brown and was stored in darkness at 4 ℃.

A thin layer of cells was formed by applying 10 μL 108 CFU·mL−1 of bacteria suspensions onto a quartz glass slide. Once the slide was completely dried naturally, the cells were attached to the slide by consecutively passing it through the flame three times. Then, 200 μL of 1.16 mg·mL−1 CDs solution diluted by BR buffer was employed to coat the bacterial region on the glass slide. Following a 1-minute incubation at room temperature, the slides were thoroughly washed with water and dried naturally. Ultimately, the cover glass was positioned onto it before being imaged by CLSM. The images were obtained by applying excitation wavelengths of 405 nm, 488 nm and 561 nm, correspondingly. After imaging, the FL intensity at three channels from five chosen regions of interest (ROIs) were recorded through image J software for further LDA.

To assess the efficacy of our method in distinguishing food samples, the milk, pork, clams and salted vegetable were acquired from a nearby supermarket. The preparation procedure for the pork contaminated with single bacteria of S. bogdii, S. typhimurium, E. coli O157:H7, S. aureus, L. monocytogenes and the clam and salted vegetable contaminated with V. parahemolyticus was conducted as follows:

the pork, clams and salted vegetables were minced and blended with 10 mL of sterilized PBS to form a homogeneous food mixture. A total of 100 μL of milk was diluted by 10 mL sterilized PBS. The mixtures were filtered using 0.22 μm filters, and the collected filtrates were then separately inoculated with different bacteria. The aforementioned CLSM imaging and data processing were applied to differentiate bacteria in these contaminated food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characteristics of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of the CDs was conducted through a one-step hydrothermal synthesis employing citric acid and PEI, as illustrated in Scheme 1a. The morphology and chemical structures of CDs were investigated. TEM image of CDs showed the roughly spherical morphologies in Fig. 1 a. XRD pattern showed a unique diffraction peak at approximately 21°, which is consistent with characteristics of the amorphous carbon (Fig. S1)[26]. With FTIR in Fig. 1b, a strong broad absorption bands of CDs at 3425 cm−1 indicated the presence of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations [27], the peak at 2843 cm−1 and 2970 cm−1 can be identified as stretching vibration of C-H [28], absorption bands at 1651, 1566, 1462, and 1404 cm−1 corresponded with stretching vibration of C = O [29], bending vibration of N-H [30], COO– group [25] and stretching vibration of C–N [30], respectively. Furthermore, the XPS full scan spectra disclosed the presence of three clear peaks around 284.8 eV, 399.1 eV, and 530.8 eV ascribed to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s. Specifically, C1s spectrum exhibits three peaks at 284.6 eV for C–C/C = C, 285.6 eV for C–N, and 287.3 eV for C = O, N1s spectrum is composed of three peaks centered at 398.7 eV for C-N-C, 399.5 eV for N-(C)3 and 400.4 eV for N-H, O 1 s spectrum displays two peaks at 530.6 eV for C = O and 532.1 eV for C–O, respectively (Fig. 1c, d, e, f)[25]. The findings implied that the CDs were successfully synthesized.

The optical characteristics of the CDs were analyzed by FL spectroscopies. The FL emission behaviors at different excitation wavelengths were obtained, and the results were shown in Fig. 2 a. The FL intensity exhibited an initial decline, followed by a significant increase, before declining once more. Concurrently, there was a shift towards longer wavelengths as the excitation progressively increased from 360 nm to 600 nm. These observations indicated that the FL spectra of CDs is excitation-dependent, which provided prerequisites for the subsequent development of a sensor array using CLSM three-channel of blue, green, red (noted B, G, R, corresponding to excitation wavelength of 405 nm, 488 nm and 561 nm).

In order to further investigate the multi-emissive characteristics, the emission shifts at the corresponding maximum excitation and the FL intensity at different excitation was monitored by varying concentrations of CDs. In Fig. S2, the normalized FL spectra of CDs with varying concentrations were depicted, illustrating a shift in the FL peak from blue to green at the maximum excitation wavelength. The phenomenon could be attributed to the aggregation of CDs. As the concentration increases, the aggregates progressively grow in size. Moreover, an increased number of oxygen-related groups give rise to complex surface states, which result in electron rapid relaxation from excited states to substrates, corresponding to longer wavelength emission [25,31]. Nevertheless, this shift was relatively minor. Subsequently, the emission spectra of 405 nm, 488 nm and 561 nm excitation was monitored to further investigate concentration-dependent FL intensity. Fig. 2b showed that the FL intensity of the three emission has different changing rules with varying concentration. Under blue and green channel, the FL intensity exhibited a rise-and-fall trend, while declined for the red channel as the concentration decreased, which indicated that the CDs possess more than one fluorescence centers. Initially, the enhancement of FL intensity was attributed to the rise in the number of fluorescent centers as the concentration of CDs increased, along with the formation of small aggregates. The increase in FL intensity caused by aggregation can be ascribed to the weakening of interaction between the hydrophilic groups and solvent molecules, thereby reducing the non-radiative decay of CDs. As the concentration further rose, larger aggregates were formed, intensifying the non-radiative relaxation between them, resulting in diminished FL intensities [32,33].

The aggregation process and the FL changes of the CDs in solutions of different concentrations were further confirmed by DLS and FL lifetimes. With the increase in CDs concentration from 0.30 mg·mL−1 to 2.31 mg·mL−1, there was an obvious rise in the hydrodynamic diameter from 68.69 nm to 92.89 nm (Fig. S3). Correspondingly, the FL lifetime increased from 6.54 ns to 11.07 ns as shown in Fig. 2c, which further verified the FL properties are related to the aggregation and size of CDs [17,25]. Based on the above results, the as-prepared CDs exhibited multiple-emissive sites and unique excitation and concentration-dependent FL, endowing them multi-color emissive property.

The fluorescence of CDs exhibited remarkable stability. Fig. S4 showed good synthesis repeatability among three consecutive batches. As depicted in Fig. S5, the FL intensity of CDs remained constant when subjected to varying concentrations of NaCl (0 ∼ 1 M), indicating that CDs could be used under physical salt conditions in practical applications. Fig. S6 showed that the FL intensity displayed almost no noticeable change and a slow decrease under green channel after 60 minutes exposure to UV light, indicating the high resistance to photo-bleaching of the CDs. In Fig. S7, it is apparent that the CDs solution maintained its color and did not show any noticeable precipitation after six weeks of storage. Furthermore, the CDs solution exhibited the same FL property as before. These findings clearly demonstrated the excellent chemical stability, photostability and long-term colloidal stability of the multicolor-emissive CDs.

To establish the bacterial identification array, the multi-color emissive CDs were employed as the elements responsible for signal reporting and recognition. The CDs are enriched with cationic amino groups through the introduction of PEI, meanwhile, the bacterial surface is abundant in negatively charged saccharides, proteins, and liposomes, providing numerous anion sites [34]. This facilitates the electrostatic interaction between the CDs and the bacterial cell surface, enabling the attachment of CDs onto the bacterial surface. Based on this, a series of experiments were conducted to confirm the feasibility.

To start, S. aureus was selected as the model bacteria and the impacts of CDs on bacterial staining and imaging was examined by varying the concentrations of CDs and incubation duration. Five regions of interest (ROIs) of individual bacteria in fluorescence images were specified and the FL intensity for each bacterium were extracted at three excitation channels. The average intensity varying with CDs concentration and incubation time were presented in Fig. S8 a and b. The FL response on S. aureus reached saturation within 1 minute of incubation with CDs at a concentration of 1.16 mg·mL−1. It is worth noting that the CDs could attach to the bacterial cells in considerable quantities and rapidity. Then, to investigate the availability of the proposed multi-color emissive CDs for bacterial discrimination, two kinds of bacteria (Gram-positive S. aureus and Gram-negative E. coli O157:H7) as models were stained with CDs and visualized using CLSM. Distinguishable FL emission could be observed between S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 confocal images of three excitations. Specifically, S. aureus exhibited a stronger fluorescent signal compared with E. coli. (Fig. S9), which could be ascribed to the formation of the bacteria - CDs hybrids with different affinity.

The differentiation of foodborne pathogens greatly relies on the surface properties of CDs and bacteria, and it has been proved that the surface properties of CDs and bacteria can be adjusted by altering the pH, leading to diverse interactions between bacteria and CDs [35,36]. Hence, FL emission behavior of CDs solution was monitored at various pH buffers (from pH 3 to 7) as exhibited in Fig. S10. A notable variation in the FL intensity was detected in specific pH solutions. The FL intensity showed its highest value in an acidic environment and its lowest value in a neutral environment. The variation could be explained by the presence of pyridinic on the surface of CDs. The pyridinic nitrogen, located on the outer side of the pyridinic ring and possessing a lone pair of electrons, significantly contributes to the non-radiative recombination process that diminishes the fluorescence through a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism. Hence, at lower pH levels, the lone pair of electrons interacts with protons, forming protonation complexes that obstruct the PET pathway [26,37]. Consequently, this facilitates a FL increase through the generation of local emission states. On the contrary, at higher pH levels, the deprotonation process enables the pyridinic nitrogen to regain its lone pair of electrons, thus activating the PET pathway and resulting in the quenching of CDs’ FL intensity from pH 3 to 7. Subsequently, the interaction of CDs with S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 at various pH buffers were further confirmed by zeta potential analysis (Fig. S11). The CDs in BR buffers of different pH exhibited various surface charge properties, being positively charged at pH 3 and slightly negatively charged at pH 7. The zeta potential of S. aureus is more negative compared to E. coli. Remarkably, the protonated CDs, at pH 3, showed higher electrostatic interaction with S. aureus than E. coli. It could be inferred that at pH 3, more protonated-CDs came closer to S. aureus aligning well with the imaging results. Furthermore, with the increase in pH from 3 to 7, the nitrogen-rich functional group present on the surface of CDs gets de-protonated. This hindered the electrostatic interaction between CDs and bacteria, thus affecting the staining efficiency of CDs with bacteria cells. These findings created favorable conditions for multi-emissive CDs to discriminate between multiple bacteria through one-step staining.

To explore the enhancement of the discriminatory capabilities of the multicolor-emissive CDs in bacterial identification assay with incorporating various pH of BR solutions into this array, a set of pH-excitation combinations (PnE, listed in Table S1) were designed as pre-screening array elements. And six kinds of bacteria including E. coli O157:H7, L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, S. bogdii, S. typhimurium and V. parahemolyticus were discriminated as shown in Fig. 3 . The confocal images of bacteria stained with different pH of BR solutions containing CDs were obtained and the FL intensity of five ROIs of individual bacteria was recorded and subjected to analysis using a supervised pattern recognition algorithm LDA. When the elements of P7E, P47E, P57E, P67E were used, several bacteria exhibited partial overlap in the LDA plot (Fig. 3a and c ∼ e) and lower accuracy (Fig. 3f). However, upon using P37E, the jackknife classification matrix indicated a high accuracy of 96.7 % in categorizing the six bacteria, which were well distinguished from each other in the two-dimensional space (Fig. 3b). The results might be ascribed to the reason that significant disparities in solution pH resulted in dissimilar attributes of the bacterial and CDs interfaces, which consequently prompted more diverse interactions, substantially enhancing the selectivity of the sensor array. Eventually, the six elements of pH 3-B, pH 3-G, pH 3-R, pH 7-B, pH 7-G, pH 7-R made up the bacterial identification array.

To further validate the capabilities of the selected array elements to recognize bacteria, the CLSM with higher resolution was employed for imaging at pH 3 and 7 respectively, confocal images of stained bacteria were presented in Fig. 4 a and b. Each bacterium showed a different FL response level to each of the six sensing elements as depicted in radar maps, demonstrating the capability of this array in turning target bacteria into the unique FL responsive differential profiling (Fig. 4c and d). To statistically explore the FL response pattern of CDs-based array towards various bacteria, the corresponding average FL intensity values were analyzed by LDA, which transformed the training matrix (P7E
3 elements × 6 bacteria × 5 replicates, P37E

6 elements × 6 bacteria × 5 replicates) into canonical factors, and the first two canonical factors were visualized in a two-dimensional plot. Regarding the element of P7E, although distinctive differentiation among most bacteria was achieved, there existed partial overlap between S. typhimurium and E. coli O157:H7 with an accuracy of 93.3 % (Fig. 4e). However, by the element of P37E, the classification accuracy could be further improved to 100 %. This is evidenced by the clear spatial separation of all six bacteria in the two-dimensional space as illustrated in Fig. 4f, emphasizing the superior differentiation capability and reproducibility of the assay. To further illustrating the simplicity and rapidity of our methods for multiple bacteria discrimination, this method was compared with the previously reported sensor arrays in terms of array elements, microorganism species and detection time (Table S2). Especially in the detection process, the short incubation time (1 minute), fast imaging and data collection, greatly shorten the entire analysis time and improve the detection efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability of the bacterial identification array

ce:section.ce:para: Identifying complex bacteria mixture is significant and challenging due to the coexistence of multiple foodborne bacteria in real environments. Herein, a mixture containing six kinds of bacteria with the concentration of 108 CFU/mL was designed to simulated bacterial coexistence, in which S. aureus, L. monocytogenes and V. parahemolyticus were employed as the model for analysis as these strains are easily distinguishable in morphology. By observing bacterial morphology under CLSM, the specific strains could be identified and the validity of LDA could be confirmed. The mixture underwent staining using 1.16 mg·mL−1 of CDs at pH 7 and examined by CLSM. Confocal images of the stained bacteria with three excitation channels were shown in Fig. 5 a to c, and the relative FL intensity of three ROIs of the three kinds of bacteria labelled with different colored arrows was recorded and associated with the appropriate LDA group based on the training matrix of P7E. All the chosen bacteria match well with their established areas in the canonical score plot (Fig. 5d) and a jackknifed classification matrix showed 100% correct discrimination among S. aureus, L. monocytogenes and V. parahemolyticus (Table S3).

To evaluate the FL emission stability of CDs, the effects of multiple variables in food samples were investigated including FeCl2, FeCl3, CuCl2, CaCl2, KCl, NaCl, MgCl2, glycine, histidine, tryptophan, cysteine, N-acetylcysteine, tyrosine, glutathione and BSA in PBS, and no obvious interference in FL intensity was observed (Fig. S12). It could be inferred that the CDs demonstrate robust resistance against interference, ensuring that FL imaging remains undisturbed even in complex food matrices.

Moreover, the assay’s suitability in actual intricate matrices was tested by conducting a trial to distinguish between six kinds of bacteria in contaminated food (milk, ground pork, clams and salted vegetables). Confocal images of bacteria when exposed to food samples matrix interference were shown in Fig. S13. The identification of bacterial contaminated samples was performed by attributing the relative FL intensity to the appropriate LDA group based on the training matrix of P37E. As revealed in Fig. 6 and Table S4, it was evident that the assay exhibited promising aptitude for identifying and distinguishing the six bacteria strains in milk, ground pork, clams and salted vegetables samples with an accuracy of 100 %.

A novel bacterial identification array was constructed by utilizing the staining and imaging capabilities of multicolor-emissive CDs, which exploited pH-dependent binding variations to discriminate different bacteria strains. The imaging experiments using CLSM was conducted, and the FL intensity data collected from the images were analyzed using LDA within minutes, enabling the accurate differentiation of six bacterial species with 100 % classification accuracy. Furthermore, the practicability of this method was confirmed by its applications in identifying bacteria in mixtures and real food samples. Overall, the simple, accurate and rapid method serves as an efficient tool for the identification of multiple foodborne bacteria simultaneously using single multi-emissive CDs, and meanwhile it expands the application potential of CLSM in high-throughput multi-target analysis.

Hang Li: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Shuo Yao: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Xuechen Wang: Formal analysis. Hui Xu: Visualization. Chao Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Funding acquisition. Juan Li: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Juan Wang: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 82073557, 82273686 and 82373628), Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Plan Item (Grant No. 20230203153SF), Program of “Medicine + X” Interdisciplinary Innovation Team of Bethune Medical Department (Grant No. 2022JBGS09) and Norman Bethune Program of Jilin University (2023B37).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109719.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
